coredata.dc:description: This study aims to intensify reactivity via bespoke transient hydrodynamics in a plasma-activated three-phase catalyst system. Likewise, three-phase plasma systems in literature are not well designed, understood and commercially available. The synthesised N-doped carbon quantum dots, with application as fertilisers and wastewater treatment was evaluated as a model reaction. A plasma microjet was produced using a commercial plasma system, creating an almost flat, large interface covering a thin stagnant liquid layer with a catalyst bed underneath. In this hydrodynamic regime, plasma can penetrate the catalyst bed via the stagnant thin liquid film and polarise the plasma-liquid interface. The new process regime’s effectiveness is proven by the enhanced reaction rate achieved by raising liquid diffusivity toward the catalyst bed through solvent viscosity reduction. Results indicate that the reaction rate depends on the small surface area interacted with the plasma jet amid an excess of excited species, not the total gas–liquid interface area. The plasma process competes energetically with the best dielectric barrier discharge processes and uses less energy than microfluidic processing in chemical microreactors. High momentum transfer from the plasma jet to the liquid partly evaporates non-water additives, impacting process design and requiring reduction.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental setup with plasma-liquid reactor and interfacial profile analytics.
(a) Schematic view of procedures applied for the calculation of evaporation and reaction rates in the synthesis of NCQDs; (b) Concentration −dependent measurements of photoluminescence intensity.
Hydrodynamic regimes by different depth of penetration of submerged microjet plasma into a liquid microvolume; termed distant (left), contact (middle), and deflection (right), [1].

Schematic of the crater profile created by the submerged plasma microjet into a microvolume, showing hydrodynamic and reactor regions that determine the chemical engineering science. a) schematic profile as per experiments in this manuscript; b) same as a), yet with fixed bed support; c) same as a), yet with larger inner liquid layer to allow convection; d) same as a), yet with two jets placed at the outer part of the crater (instead of one jet in the crater centre); and e) schematics to define total surface area and specific surface area of inner liquid layer.
Gas velocity of the microjet plasma with distance; using the settings of the neoplas industrial plasma system [10].
Stagnant layer thickness with gas velocity. Liquid: water. Deflection mode.

Plasma-liquid interface profile for pure water (viscosity: 1.002 mPa.s), water/ethanol 1:1 (viscosity: 1.005 mPa.s), glycerol/water 1:3 (viscosity: 253.42 mPa.s), glycerol-water–ethanol (viscosity 127.27 mPa.s), water/surfactant 10-3 mol/l (viscosity: 1.01 mPa.s), and water/surfactant/ethanol (viscosity 1.055 mPa.s). The height of zero corresponds to the gas–liquid interface without plasma jet. The measured line of the water surface cross-section, at its deepest point, refers to the top of the stagnant layer.
Water-surfactant vs water interface profiles above the inner liquid layer. Deflection mode. The height of zero corresponds to the gas–liquid interface without plasma jet.
Total surface area at different liquid properties; taken for all three hydrodynamic regimes with distinct plasma crater profiles.

Schematic of the (experimentally determined) crater profile created by the submerged plasma microjet into a microvolume for systematic physico-chemical variation, determining hydrodynamics and reactor regions. Deflection mode.
Surface of the inner aqueous layer, depending on additives given to modify the physico-chemical properties (density, viscosity, surface tension). Deflection mode.
Surface profile of (a) pure water, (b) glycerol and (c) glycerol-ethanol mixture as compared with surface without plasma treatment at the deflection mode.
Reaction rate as a function of the distance from the nozzle in three configurations. Liquid: water-glycerol mixture.
Reaction rate as a function of reciprocal viscosity for the three jet configurations. Liquid: water-glycerol mixture.

a) Reaction rate of the synthesis reaction of NCQDs with and without Ni80Cr20 catalysts for various solvents (pure water, glycerol + water, and glycerol + ethanol) in three configurations. b) Glycerol + water system: reaction rate, with and without Ni80Cr20 catalysts as a function of the total surface area.
Influence of solvents on evaporation and reaction rate in the synthesis of NCQDs operated at deflection mode.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study aims to intensify reactivity via bespoke transient hydrodynamics in a plasma-activated three-phase catalyst system. Likewise, three-phase plasma systems in literature are not well designed, understood and commercially available. The synthesised N-doped carbon quantum dots, with application as fertilisers and wastewater treatment was evaluated as a model reaction. A plasma microjet was produced using a commercial plasma system, creating an almost flat, large interface covering a thin stagnant liquid layer with a catalyst bed underneath. In this hydrodynamic regime, plasma can penetrate the catalyst bed via the stagnant thin liquid film and polarise the plasma-liquid interface. The new process regime’s effectiveness is proven by the enhanced reaction rate achieved by raising liquid diffusivity toward the catalyst bed through solvent viscosity reduction. Results indicate that the reaction rate depends on the small surface area interacted with the plasma jet amid an excess of excited species, not the total gas–liquid interface area. The plasma process competes energetically with the best dielectric barrier discharge processes and uses less energy than microfluidic processing in chemical microreactors. High momentum transfer from the plasma jet to the liquid partly evaporates non-water additives, impacting process design and requiring reduction.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Alike for highly productive chemical microreactors, applying process intensification principles to small plasma-chemical reaction volumes is essential to amplify productivity. On that backdrop, this submerged plasma jet study reports deciphering the engineering fundamentals of how to enhance mass transport and facilitate catalysis via a metal particle bed of alloy particles to increase reaction rate; beyond the performance achieved in [1]. Leverage is provided via bespoke hydrodynamics when penetrating the jet deeper into the reactor volume, displacing the liquid and demonstrating a massive increase in productivity [1]. Depending on the jet penetration into the liquid medium depth, different crater configurations (distant, contact, deflection) form in the liquid, each with different hydrodynamics. Leverage is also provided via the achievement of a gas (plasma)–liquid–solid three-phase contact for facilitating catalysis, for which a mechanism was proposed based on excited state analytics using optical emission spectroscopy [2].

Reaction kinetics is critical in chemical and biochemical processes [3,4]. It's important to customize hydrodynamics to optimize intrinsic kinetics rather than merely achieving kinetics with reduced effectiveness. Learning can be taken from the principles of high-intensity gas–liquid two-phase reactors [5]. Applications of plasma jet reactors exposed to liquid media have been reported for applications, including wastewater treatment [6] and nanoparticle synthesis [7]. However, the engineering and hydrodynamic aspects of these reactors have not been extensively explored. Previous studies demonstrated that evaporation plays a key role in plasma-liquid systems which motivated the evaporation investigation reported herein [8,9].

This study examines how altering liquid phase properties affects hydrodynamics and mass transport, both with and without a catalyst bed of alloy flakes. It is also investigated if charged plasma species affecting the bottom layer could further boost reaction kinetics. A commercial plasma equipment enables facile experimentation, the KINPen®IND reactor, providing a cold atmospheric-pressure microplasma jet with noble gases such as argon [10,11]. This microplasma jet reactor has attracted considerable literature studies and documentation [5,12,13].

As test reaction the formation of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a recently discovered “zero-dimensional” carbon nanomaterial [14–17], which have applications in various fields such as agriculture [18,19], environment [20,21], biology [22,23], chemistry [24], food science [25,26], and energy [27–30], was selected. This is due to their favourable properties, such as low toxicity [31], intense luminescence [32], chemical stability [33], biocompatibility [34], and high specific surface area [35]. Extensive research studies on N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) have been conducted, reporting a large number of diverse structures of this nanomaterial class with distinct properties, made via various synthetic techniques such as pyrolysis [36], hydrothermal [37], solvothermal [38], microwave [39], chemical vapor deposition [40], electrochemical method [41], high-temperature combustion [42], etc.

A challenge still is the capacity for pilot or industrial production of these materials and how to fill this technology gap [43]; this paper explores fundamentals to transition. A techno-sustainability study compared six NCQD production methods, highlighting plasma synthesis as the most energy-efficient but least mass-efficient, resulting in lower productivity, partly due to the use of larger reactors. The approach of this study is to use a small reactor that can be numbered up, with high volume-specific productivity, both because of high mass and energy efficiency [44]. This submerged plasma microjet was previously shown to reach productivities providing product weights in the gram scale, that is sufficient to provide a targeted, marketable quantity of nanomaterials in a few hours [1], compared to the best reactors in the field [43].

Note that in our previous studies [1], plasma species produced in this system and the reaction scheme are presented. This work shows analysis of radical species of N-doped carbon quantum dots synthesis using OES. CH• and:C = C: fragments dominate for carbon besides O- and H-related species. NH• (amino radicals) and •CN (cyano radicals) are found at 336 nm and 379 nm, respectively. •CN radicals are the primary source to form nitrogen defects in the form of graphitic and pyrrolic nitrogen. In contrast, NH• radicals are the fundamental species to introduce –NH2 groups into the surface of N-doped carbon dots.

These works also show the mechanism of the microplasma synthesis of N-doped CDs, which is in agreement with the exciting species found in the OES analysis. After folic acid solution is exposed to microplasma, the chemical structure is decomposed into intermediate reactive species such as CH•,:C = C:, •CN, OH•, NH•. Then, these reactive radicals will react together to nucleate larger radicals, i.e. phenyl, pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen radicals. In the growth stage, phenyl, pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen radicals collide and aggregate to form a single layer of graphene sheet-like structure with pyrrolic and graphitic nitrogen defects as well as –NH2 functional groups. Finally, these sheets will gather together layer by layer to form a new stable structure with spherical morphology, which is N-doped carbon quantum dots.

The goal of this study is to understand how the interface between the plasma and the liquid is deformed and how that might be utilised to increase mass transport. The latter effect is monitored via the NCQDs forming reaction rate (without presence of catalyst). In particular, the effect of diffusivity of the liquid reactant on the reaction rate is studied by changing the liquid viscosity. The effect of other physical parameters is also investigated, including density, polarity, and surface tension; in pure form and as combined effect. With that understanding, a fixed bed is placed on the floor of the reaction vessel, and operated in a mode that the bed is fully wetted and not dried out by the impinging plasma microjet. When using metal flakes as bed, the goal is to check for catalytic activity. In addition, the extent of evaporation of the liquid component is investigated, as Bruggeman et al. reported that evaporation plays a key role in plasma-liquid two-phase systems. Volatile components are added to change the physico-chemical parameters of the liquid phase [45].

Plasma gas jet-liquid processing approaches were reported for plasma-wastewater research [46,47] and plasma-biomedical research [48]. Some of those approaches use a plasma jet, as we do, which creates a crater in a liquid. Those papers aim at applications, our study does not aim and is fundamental in nature. Our study aims for fundamental understanding of hydrodynamics and engineering of a new plasma-microfluidic concept; increase in reactivity, as reported earlier, is only a tool to demonstrate this.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical parameter variation of the liquid reaction medium
ce:section.ce:para: In order to understand the influence of gas–liquid interfacial area and the diffusivity of the liquid reactant on the reaction rate, several solvents with different density and viscosity were used as listed in Table 1 .

The experimental design is based on the idea of high-intensity gas–liquid reactors that are equipped with forceful gas jets, which are known to enhance phase mixing [49]. Utilized in the experiment was a downward vertical plasma jet with a velocity of 16 m s−1. This jet entered a compact reactor volume through a nozzle with a diameter of 1.6 mm. The distances of 10 mm, 1 mm, and 0 mm from the nozzle to the liquid are investigated in our work. Upon reaching the lower end, the jet changed course and proceeded to travel upward along the gas–liquid interface. The plasma stream forms a crater in the liquid, resulting in a highly turbulent movement of liquid inside the reactor volume due to gas to liquid momentum transfer. At Reynolds numbers exceeding 300 in microcavities, the hydrodynamics exhibit the engulfment regime. This phenomenon occurs when convection in a direction orthogonal to the main flow contributes to turbulence.

Another high-intensity gas–liquid reactor design includes a pump, an orifice, a vertical tube aligned with the orifice, and a baffle plate [49]. The pump produces a downward vertical jet of liquid through the orifice, generating a gas–liquid dispersion in the tube. The two-phase jet is directed onto a baffle near the bottom of the tank, distributing it throughout the liquid. The liquid is then collected and recycled under the baffle plate. The published high-intensity gas–liquid reactor concepts cannot be taken fully as is in this experimental design, but need adaptation for the fact that plasma is not long living and cannot exist over long distances to the liquid phase; which is the motivation and quintessence of this study proposing the following. Instead, a micro-plasma jet aimed at the liquid is utilised, as shown in Fig. 1 . The flow feedback for repeated gas–liquid interactions is also withheld, as the fundamental nature of the process intensification is better demonstrated through single contact [49].

The experimental synthesis process and reactor configuration were taken, as reported in previous studies [1]. The synthesis reactor was comprised of two parts, a kINPen®IND microplasma jet (Neoplas GmbH, Germany), and a cylindrical glass reactor with a diameter of 15 mm and height of 9 mm, designed and manufactured in-house. A 110 VAC power supply (50 Hz) was used to connect with the kINPen®IND microplasma jet. Argon, with a purity of 99.9 vol%, was fed with a mass flow rate of 2 L per minute (l min−1) via the nozzle with a mean velocity of 16 m s−1. The jet length was 1 cm. Folic acid, and all other chemicals (for modifying the physical properties from the aqueous phase) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Regarding kINPen®IND microplasma jet employed in this study and explained in a previous work [50], this plasma jet configuration consists of an inner needle electrode, powered and centrally positioned within a capillary with a diameter of 1.6 mm. The inner electrode is operated at a frequency of approximately 0.9 MHz, with a peak-to-peak voltage of 2.1 kV. The visible plasma effluent extends to a length of around 10 mm to 12 mm. Within the same plasma jet apparatus, another studies of Bleker et al. [51] utilized calorimetric power deposition by combining Schlieren measurements with computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations, resulting in a power deposition of 1.1 Watts. Furthermore, in this experimental setup, the gas temperature was determined through CFD simulations and Schlieren measurements, yielding a temperature of approximately 300 K in the regions exterior to the plasma plume. The electron densities within the streamer head were anticipated to reach levels on the order of 1014 cm−3, with electron temperatures reaching up to 4 eV. Further details about this plasma jet are available at review of Reuter et al. [10]. It is important to note that the experimental conditions described in this paper closely resembled those presented in the referenced study.
Advanced physical characterization of the carbon quantum dots used in this study have been reported in terms of size, crystallinity, composition, surface groups, and more [1,52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial area of the crater and the liquid layer at the bottom
ce:section.ce:para: Calculation of specific surface area: When the plasma jet hits the liquid, a crater is formed. The total area of the crater was analysed by image analysis [53], Fig. 1. It was assumed that the crater has an axisymmetrical shape. The total interfacial area was calculated from the three-dimensional surface plots, assuming the plasma gas recess is symmetrical around the central plasma microjet; meaning seeing this recess from above it has spherical shape. The curve describing the interface was fitted using a polynomial function of degree 4 to the experimental curve obtained from the image analysis. The gas–liquid interface area of the bottom liquid layer was estimated by a simplified method. The shape of the bottom liquid layer below the crater, typically with a thickness in the 200–300 µm range, was approximated with straight lines to the polynomial curve describing the crater shape. Then the area was calculated from the obtained shape of the thin liquid layer below the crater.

Calculation of stagnant layer thickness: The equations (1) to (4) are derived from the film theory; see e.g. in [54]. The theory considers that resistance to mass transfer in a turbulent fluid phase is present in a thin layer adjacent to an interface. Water properties are assumed. Viscosity is μ = 0.001 Pa s, density is ρ = 1000 kg m−3. Then, the thickness δ of the stagnant layer (L) is given as follows, equation (1) (Eq (1).

An aqueous solution containing 20 μL of folic acid (0.1 g/mL) and 1 ml of a given liquid was transferred to the glass reactor (Fig. 2 a). The microplasma jet was vertically directed towards the reactor at three configurations: (i) 'distant' (jet in contact with liquid with a weak momentum transfer), (ii) 'contact' (jet colliding deep into liquid, with a moderate impact on hydrodynamics), and (iii) 'deflection' (jet perforating liquid completely down to reactor floor; a strong momentum transfer) [2], Fig. 3 . After 10 min, the microplasma treatment of the liquid was stopped. The final volume of liquid was used to calculate the evaporation rate, Fig. 2a.

The fluorescence emission is one of the most unique features of NCQDs, utilised in many fields [55]. In this study, fluorescence emission is used for calculating the reaction rate, Fig. 2a. Our previous studies have shown no evidence for a size increase [1,52], yet finally it cannot be excluded that the increased photoluminescence is not only due to an increase in number of quantum dots but also due to their size increase. The paper reports on means to productivity per ‘weight of produced product’.

To apply a correlation between measured photoluminescence and conversion, it needs to be excluded that carbon nanodots, synthesised in the different water-solvent systems in this study, have different size, shape, and morphology. This would mean that a photoluminescence spectrum of one carbon nanodot synthesised in one water-solvent system cannot be compared to another carbon nanodot.

By transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurements we determined that the error by different solvation of the nanodots can be regarded as non-significant for the conclusions drawn in this study, Fig. S2 in the Supplementary Information (SI). The shown conversion graphs report order of magnitude differences of the conversion determined, while the six solvent systems tested show similar photoluminescence with ± 12.5 % deviation. In addition, the error by different size and shape is regarded as well as non-significant for three (out of four) syntheses in different solvent systems; with the information of the fourth one regarded still to be sufficient to give a semi-quantitative correlation, Fig. S3 and Table S1. This means that the impact of the solvent systems on the photoluminescence, both by solvation or by the nanodot synthesis, is mainly or largely determined by their change in physical properties (viscosity, density, polarity, and surface tension) of the reaction system, as intended by the study.

After 10 min, the jet was stopped, and the colour of the solution changed from orange to brownish. The solution was analyzed with a photoluminescence spectrometer (FLS 1000, UK). The excitation and emission wavelengths were set at 360 and 450 nm, respectively. The photoluminescence intensity of NCQDs was used to obtain the reaction rate. In several experiments, 0.5 g of Ni80Cr20 alloy flakes were placed at the bottom. The size of the catalyst particles was 500 µm. The catalyst particles were fixed to the reactor floor under plasma operation and the plasma gas jet impetus was unable to move these with the liquid.

No special fixation was needed for the catalyst flakes at the bottom of the reactor. Their size, weight, and entanglement were sufficient to keep them in place even under the large plasma gas flows. All video and photographic imaging did not show any catalyst displacement.

Photoluminescence 3D colour mapping determined the maximal excitation and emission wavelength of the produced N-doped carbon dots to ensure best signal validity recorded. These parameter values were used for further photoluminescence intensity tests as described in [1,52]. We found a linear dependence when concentrations and reaction times were varied up to a certain threshold, as reported in [1]. At higher concentrations the relationship levelled off, possibly due to nonradiative processes [56], wavelength redshifts, and quenching [57]. This study does not operate in those process regimes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrodynamics for process intensification created by microjet plasma
ce:section.ce:para: Different depth of penetration of the plasma microjet into the liquid microvolume creates genuine hydrodynamic regimes, characterised by a large interfacial area, and the presence of a thin 'inner' liquid layer, at the floor of the reactor, with a moderate interfacial area. A catalyst can be added on the bottom as a fixed bed.

In the ‘distant’ position, the tip of the microplasma jet, which is the most protruding part, does not contact the liquid surface and only generates a small crater. In the ‘contact’ position, the plasma jet penetrates deeper, forming a liquid film above the catalyst surface. The ‘deflection’ position resembles the “fast impinging jet reactor,” a plasma reactor configuration proposed by Botes [5]. In this configuration, the liquid film is further thinned, and the plasma gas stream is redirected harshly, creating turbulence in the plasma-gas phase and potentially recirculation in the bulk liquid; intensifying mass transport in both phases. As a result, a multiphase system with a continuous renewal of the interface is expected, possibly assisted by the generation and breakup of small droplets from the water surface, as reported in the literature [58].

It has been discovered that the highest intensity of charged (activated) species, which are mentioned above, can be detected in the ‘deflection’ position [2]. The ‘deflection’ position is chosen as prime hydrodynamic setting for the study reported here, while the two other positions are co-investigated.

The new plasma microjet concept can compete energy-wise well with DBD plasma reactors. Only 1 W is consumed for generating of the micro jet plasma in this study [10], and pumping a gas flow rate of 2.0 L/h consumes about 6 W [59], together 7 W. This is energy consumption for a single-run; the production of significant amount of material requires several runs; in the order of about 5–10 runs. Accordingly, with a consumption of a few ten watts, gram quantities of nanomaterial can be produced. Yang et al. studied dielectric barrier discharge-accelerated one-pot synthesis of sulfur quantum dots for fluorescent sensing of lead ions and l-cysteine. The authors completed plasma assisted synthesis in 20 min but they do not provide energy efficiency or electric characteristics of their process [60]. Reniers et al. developed a simple method for the fabrication of nitrogen doped titanium dioxide by using NH3 gas as both discharge gas and doping precursors, and NH3 concentration played a key role in controlling the doping degree [61]. With the doping of nitrogen, the band gap of N–TiO2 was obviously reduced, resulting in enhanced photocatalytic activity for dye degradation. Qiu et al. studied and compared the modification effects of different discharge gases on the same nanomaterial [62]. They modified TiO2 nanosheets for enhanced visible light photocatalytic activity by DBD plasma. The DBD-plasma was initiated under 60 V and 1.2 A AC input power at room temperature for 10 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of physical parameters of the liquid on the crater shape
ce:section.ce:para: The plasma microjet displaces a major part of the liquid volume. It establishes a void gas volume that is characterized by a high-velocity gas downward that impinges on the liquid surface to be redirected upwards out of the void volume. Strong convection can be expected under those conditions ensuring good mass transport of the plasma to the gas–liquid interface. Relevant is that the use of the microjet secures that plasma contacts the liquid interface and potentially the catalyst beneath it. The high gas velocity transfers its momentum to the liquid phase to create a liquid recirculation loop, Fig. 4 . This liquid convection refreshes reactant in the stagnant layer from the ‘feed’ of the non-stagnant convective liquid zone; Fig. 4a.

Note that in chemical engineering, hydrodynamic and reactor regions play crucial roles in determining the behavior and performance of reaction process. The hydrodynamic domain denotes the segment within a chemical reactor where the behavior of fluids (liquids or gases) is predominantly regulated by fluid dynamics principles. Conversely, the reactor region refers to the section of the chemical reactor where the actual chemical reactions occur, leading to the transformation of reactants into desired products.

The stagnant liquid layer is controlled by diffusion, which is a slow process. It would be desirable setting the stagnant layer to smaller dimensions, down to a few micrometers, when diffusion is fast enough to provide mass transfer to the catalyst. Yet our calculations show this is not feasible. Alternatively, a rough surface of a bed of Ni80Cr20 flakes underneath the stagnant liquid layer may introduce turbulence and therefore increase convection towards the catalyst; Fig. 4b.

Another concept might be ‘convectional pumping’ of the plasma-activated species close to the catalyst; Fig. 4c. A layer thicker than the velocity boundary layer thickness (δ) allows a convective flow to enter into the ‘inner liquid surface area’, directly exposed to the plasma jet. As given in Fig. 4c, this would actually pump the plasma-activated species away from the supposed reaction zone at the catalyst. This demands for a new positioning of the plasma jet from the centre position to an outside one; Fig. 4d. A tilted arrangement of the jets is needed to achieve maximum transfer of gas momentum to the liquid. In this configuration, the convection flow would transfer plasma-excited species to the liquid stagnant (‘reaction’) zone and catalyst. Nonetheless, the configuration of two jets was not employed in this paper; instead, this aspect is currently under development, and we intend to present its findings in a subsequent paper.

Roughening of the stagnant liquid–solid interface would increase efficiency of the concepts of the stagnant layer, as in Fig. 4a) and the convective layer, as in Fig. 4b. As shown in the magnified image of Fig. 4b, the catalyst bed with intertwined flakes has surface deviation in the order of a few mm, and therefore can roughen and disrupt the ‘stagnant layer’. A plain stagnant later will likely not form under those conditions, as predicted by fluid dynamic theory for an ideally flowing system with plain walls. This layer disruption allows the catalyst to have direct surface contact to the plasma and the reactive species, while the reaction remains diffusion-limited by species transport in the liquid phase.

The surface profiles in the three positions were recorded, investigating phenomenonally the shape of the interface and its total surface area and its specific surface area below the crater. The optimum configuration for interface shape would be a rather flat surface with a large surface area near the reactor floor (the potential position for a catalytic bed), Fig. 4e.

The new process design of the two-jet system, as given in Fig. 4d, might be also superior to the other designs shown in Fig. 4 by minimizing the otherwise unavoidable contact of the backwards gas flow (which likely comprises no plasma-activated species) to the water crater surface. This is possible by placing the two plasma jets out of the liquid volume centre, leaving a larger central conduct for the backwards gas flow to leave.

To examine the magnitude of the effect, calculations of the gas velocity were done as a function of distance from the plasma-device outlet [10], as shown in Fig. 5 . Basic assumptions are (i) the velocity of air outside the plasma jet is considered as zero; (ii) temperature of gas is considered 300 K; (iii) the diameter of nozzle is considered as 1.6 mm [10]; and the air flow rate is considered as 2 L.min−1.

The pressure decreases when gas leaves the nozzle, and its velocity increases, in line with the Bernoulli's principle, Fig. 5. Then, the gas velocity decreases downstream the nozzle due to frictional losses and volumetric expansion of the jet.

The thickness of the stagnant layer can be calculated by common chemical engineering formulas. Motivation is to determine if diffusion might be effective; meaning how long diffusion might take, noting that for our study fastest diffusion is needed in the order of milliseconds and less. The calculation by (Eq. (1), provides the thickness of the stagnant layer as a function of the gas velocity. The formula pertains to liquid velocity, which was derived from the conservation of momentum at the gas–liquid interface (Eq. (3). At the high gas velocity of around 15–16 m s−1, relevant for this study, the stagnant layer is expected to be ca. 0.6 mm. Such distance relates to diffusion times of several minutes, meaning the plasma-activated species will not be able to reach the catalyst, Fig. 6 . Diffusion follows Fick’s first law [63]. In dilute aqueous solutions the diffusion coefficients of most species are in the range of (0.6–2) × 10 − 9 m2/s at room temperature. Assuming a stagnant layer of a few millimeters thickness, as we calculated, corresponds to diffusion times in the order of 103 s, which is several minutes.

Note that Fig. 6 illustrates the boundary layer thickness in the case of water. In all other cases, the theoretical boundary layer thickness exceeds the geometrical distance between the jet and the catalyst.

This shows the need to roughen the catalyst surface, as presented in Fig. 4b. Stagnant layers are known to form on smooth, plain surfaces [64,65]. Their existence or change in properties on rough surfaces is unknown. The catalyst surface topography fluctuation is in the order of a few millimetres, meaning in the same order as the thickness of the stagnant layer itself.

To widen from using pure water only, glycerol, ethanol and sodium dodecyl sulfate (surfactant) were added to water to change viscosity, polarity, and surface tension of the liquid, respectively, and to study their impact on the crater surface area, liquid stagnant layer area and diffusivity of the reactant. The exact compositions of those solutions are given in Table 1. The interface shape for different set of parameters (as shown in Fig. 7 ) was recorded and discussed below. The reactor possesses an asymmetrical shape, resulting in its length being equivalent to its width. Fig. 7 displays a singular cross-section of the reactor.

For pure water, the above given criterion of a good surface profile is better met for the contact mode as compared to the distant mode, with slight further improvement in the deflection mode. A ‘good surface profile’ comprises a stagnant layer of lowest thickness, constant thickness, and large width (i.e., surface area). Adding glycerol to the aqueous solutions increases viscosity and slightly changes density. The impact on the desired process design is low, meaning the surface profiles are similar to those with pure water. The interfacial surface area increases from 238 to 249 mm2, when moving to the deflection regime, Fig. 7.

Adding ethanol alters polarity and adding surfactant adjusts surface tension. The profiles obtained are characterised by nearly vertical walls approaching a vertical well shape with a thin aqueous layer between the gas and the solid wall. This well shape is kept and the vertical alignment of the side walls improved when combining both or combining these with the glycerol (high viscosity) addition; for surfactant-ethanol (low surface tension – high polarity) it is found for all three positions (distant, contact, deflection).

Adding ethanol and glycerol to the aqueous solutions increases both polarity and viscosity. The impact is more remarkable than changing viscosity alone. The gas–liquid interface gets more flattened, meaning the thin liquid layer has a rather constant thickness and a larger interfacial area. The large extent of flattening of the water-surfactant profile of the inner aqueous layer is evident when comparing the respective water profile; using large magnification of this 'micro-zone', as presented in Fig. 8 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of physical parameters of the liquid on total surface area
ce:section.ce:para: While the jet penetrates the liquid microvolume deeper, the total surface area of the crater is decreased to a small or medium extent (Fig. 9 ); counter-intuitive to the expectation about strong impingement effect. The total surface area is largest for the deflection mode, followed by the contact and distant mode; with the contact mode being closer to the deflection one. The total surface area of the crater is the largest for pure water, while the lowest for water-surfactant. The specific (volume-rated) surface area is for pure water ca. 250 m−1, a value typical for advanced reactor equipment, yet lower than interfaces generated in microreactors (1,000 m−1 to 10,000 m−1) [66]. The total surface is about 10 times larger than for a plain surface, non-perturbed by jet penetration.

In a classical (macro-scale) jet plasma-liquid interaction, a deeper position of the jet will penetrate deeper into the liquid. Yet in our micro-scale plasma-liquid interaction, with small dimensions and larger impact of wetting forces by the reactor surfaces, this correlation does not hold. We displace, even in the distant mode, almost the entire liquid volume out of the main central zone of the reactor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of physical parameters of the liquid on specific surface area of the inner liquid layer
ce:section.ce:para: In this section, the distinct interfacial area within the inner aqueous layer and the minimal thickness of the inner water layer at the plasma cavity's central point have been elucidated, thereby also revealing the surface shape of the inner liquid layer. An ideal surface profile for chemical processing is flat (ultrathin) and thus constant in the water thickness, likely with diffusion being the governing mechanism of mass transport. This ultrathin liquid layer may also be ideal to employ reaction amplification by a catalyst.

The real inner surface profiles are curved, yet some are close to being ideal flat (water, water/surfactant, water/surfactant/ethanol, or water/glycerol/ethanol), Fig. 10 . While the surface areas differ, as shown in Fig. 7, the average thickness of the inner water layer differs as well (Fig. 6).

The scenarios considered have mostly mixed average hydrodynamic performance, e.g. having low average thickness of the inner water layer at low surface area, and vice versa. The scenarios water and water-surfactant have the best hydrodynamics. The profiles of aqueous systems with increased viscosity or reduced surface tension are close to an ideal flat surface, while having a larger depth of the inner layer throughout, and not only at the edges. Therefore, the plasma jet-liquid system can be fine-tuned in terms of having either a thin inner liquid layer (averaged over the span) or a flat layer.

The surface of the inner aqueous layer at the bottom (Fig. 11 ) is 20–25 % of the total surface area of the crater (Fig. 7), which fits to the observation of five zones in Fig. 4 of almost equal length and curvature. The addition of ethanol allows to make it still larger than for pure water, including admixture of surfactant and glycerol, whereas the addition of (only) glycerol halve the specific surface area. Taking 28 mm2 as highest value (assuming averaged 0.1 mm layer thickness at 2.8 mm3 volume), the specific (volume-rated) surface area for pure water is ca. 10,000 m−1, a value as high than the greatest interfaces reported for microreactors with capability for high capacity (1,000 m−1 to 10,000 m−1); with up to 10,000 m−1 reported for analytical microchip devices [66]. The specific volume of the inner aqueous layer is just 0.28 % of the total volume (2.8 vs. 1000 mm3), yet is supposed to make a major effort to the reaction outcome.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of physical parameters of the liquid on the inner liquid layer in the presence of a porous solid beneath
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve both a thin and flat inner liquid layer for one liquid system, the hydrodynamic profile is determined in the presence of a porous bed, composed of particles in the order of the size of the depth of the inner layer or somewhat larger; including glass spheres or metal flakes. The liquid will penetrate that bed and the surface of the liquid is not expected to be influenced by wetting of the glass reaction vessel; assumed to cause the larger depth of the inner layer for the viscous and surface tension-reduced liquids.

The plasma jet in the deflection mode was exposed to a porous fixed bed of Ni-Cr alloy flakes draining a liquid, as shown in Fig. 12 . This changed the surface profile as compared to exposing the plasma microjet to water without fixed bed. An ideal flat profile is obtained for the glycerol (high viscosity) and glycerol-ethanol (high polarity- high viscosity) mixtures. In the case of glycerol-ethanol (high viscosity-high polarity), the surface area of the liquid inner layer is larger than for the case without fixed bed. In the case of pure water, no effect is observed. The profile is just elevated to higher position. It is suspected that high surface tension of the gas/water interface prevents to form a flat profile as given for the other two cases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of liquid viscosity on NCQDs reaction
ce:section.ce:para: This study intends to deploy the above-mentioned variation of physical properties for intensifying the chemistry of the NCQDs formation. Surface tension alteration does not show reaction rate dependency, while having best hydrodynamic surface profile with most extended surface of the thin layer. Polarity variation has no effect on the reaction rate either.

However, the NCQDs reaction rate was a strong function of viscosity and therefore diffusivity of the liquid reactant. The experiments were directed to detail this effect. The reaction rate increases as viscosity decreases and it increases largely from the distant to contact position, as demonstrated in Fig. 13 . It can be seen that the ‘deflection’ mode is best in overcoming viscosity limitation. The ‘contact’ mode is close to this performance. The ‘distant’ mode is behind the performances of the three modes.

Question is if that effect is related to unit operations (diffusivity) alone or transcribed by chemical effects (viscosity changing solvent polarity). The variation of viscosity, and the other physical parameters, do not follow the common effect of square dependency of reaction rate with co-solvent/ingredient concentration (see Supplementary Material). A chemistry-induced change of reactivity can therefore be excluded, and conclusion is the reaction rate reflect intrinsic dependencies of hydrodynamics and mass transfer on the reaction rate, i.e. diffusivity.

To further corroborate that the reaction rate is diffusion limited, the rate was plotted vs reciprocal viscosity of the solvent, which is proportional to diffusivity, Einstein's equation, as shown in Fig. 14
[67].

If the reaction was to occur in the bulk liquid, the reaction rate would theoretically be directly proportional to the gas–liquid interface area, as this is the primary factor affecting mass transfer. However, it has been observed that altering the interface area does not lead to a change in the reaction rate, indicating that the role of the bulk reaction in the overall rate is negligible. Instead, the reaction predominantly occurs within the stagnant layer near the catalyst surface. The preferential reaction zone if no catalyst is used is the stagnant layer as well. Modifying the thickness of this stagnant layer and the diffusivity of the liquid reactant has a substantial impact on the reaction rate.

It cannot fully be excluded that the variation of process parameters by adding small organic molecules has an additional influence via those molecules taking part in the reaction (and then modifying the nanodot product). Yet, from literature evidence conclusion is that impact might be low. Plasma-induced glycerol decomposition, e.g., proceeds dehydration to acetol and cracking to methanol, glycolaldehyde, and methyl methanoate. Conversion was higher in an N2 environment [68]. Plasma-induced ethanol decomposition showed acidolysis of the ethanol with hydrogen peroxide being formed [69]. While, therefore, small organic molecules possibly could have been incorporated in the carbon nanodots, these are not expected to change massively their chemical composition and fluorescent properties. This study did not work under plasma conditions that are reported to boost small molecule decomposition.

When varying process conditions in this study, we admittedly cannot rule out that the size, morphology, structure, and surface- and bulk-molecular composition of the produced nanomaterials of the produced nanomaterials may change. The needed investigations to clarify this would require an expansion of scope that is beyond what can be delivered within one study and one paper. We plan to address this in a future study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ncqds reaction in presence of a metal flakes bed: Test catalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para: A catalyst bed with the Ni-Cr alloy flakes was added. The effect of the Ni-Cr alloy catalyst to the plasma-liquid system to amplify the reaction rate was determined also for the combined viscosity-polarity effect, employing glycerol-ethanol–water mixtures. Hypothesis is that excited plasma species (Ar+, N2 +, O+, OH*) can diffuse faster to the catalyst beneath to induce the NCQDs formation [70,71]. The deflection mode is best to utilise these plasma-excited species.
A rate enhancement of about 25 % could be achieved for the catalyst-supported reaction experiment in the deflection position, as presented in Fig. 15 .

While this is not enough to justify proclaiming a catalytic effect in the classical meaning, it still marks a considerable process improvement. Of larger impact, in the order of five times intensification, the reaction rate increases from 76 to 387 (au/min) when changing the position from distant to deflection. Yet this effect can only be leveraged in pure water, meaning at low viscosity (1.002 mPa.s); the Ni-Cr catalyst cannot overrule the diffusion limitation and the formation of a relatively thicker layer above the catalyst. Yet for constant (high) viscosity, the catalyst is effective to increase reaction rate as the determined rates show for glycerol + water (252.42 mPa.s) and glycerol + ethanol (127.27 mPa.s). The catalyst effect is strongest in the deflection mode, as given for the non-catalysed experiments, Fig. 12.

When combining the data taken from Figs. 9 and 15a, the reaction rate can be given as a function of the total surface area. The water-glycerol mixture was investigated, as diffusion is much slowed down, allowing to monitor the surface area effect without much interference. The respective total surface area grows by only 10 % for the distant, contact, and deflection mode, Fig. 15b. For this variation, the reaction rate grows by about 25 % and about 20 % when using plasma with and without catalyst, respectively. For the water-glycerol mixture, viscosity is high, and diffusion consequently is low. When allowing diffusion to gain significance, by using pure water with low viscosity, the gain in reaction rate over surface area is > 500 %, Fig. 15a. That confirms that diffusion, as demonstrated in Fig. 14, and not surface area is essential for the micro jet plasma process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaporation control to prevent liquid phase media change
ce:section.ce:para: Evaporation is likely to happen when a high-velocity jet impinges on a liquid surface of large specific area. Evaporation of solvent might change reaction rate and product quality of the NCQDs nanomaterial, making necessary metering and control of evaporation. This may be facilitated by the fact that plasma commonly transfers heat to the surface of the liquid [9].

The calculated evaporation and reaction rate were determined for the NCQDs synthesis in the deflection mode, when varying the physico-chemical parameters of the liquid reaction medium, Fig. 16 . The evaporation rate for pure water, surfactant, and glycerol: water (1:3) is relatively similar, in the vicinity of 30 (µL/min). This changes when ethanol is added, a volatile solvent. The evaporation rate of water + ethanol, surfactant + ethanol, and glycerol + ethanol increased significantly, compared to reaction media without ethanol. The reaction rate decreased considerably, except for the case of glycerol + ethanol (20.49 au/min), which is almost invariant. The evaporated removal of ethanol might be one cause for the decline in reaction rate, adding to the other here reported causes, including the thickness of the inner liquid layer and the total and inner layer-related specific surface area.

Experiments demonstrate that the evaporation rate remains practically constant in all three hydrodynamic regimes within the investigated range, which is consistent with determination that the total surface area of the plasma crater only minorly varies for the regimes, Fig. 17 . The remaining difference might be due to that most evaporation takes place at the large specific surface of the inner aqueous layer, which is practically invariant to changes of the hydrodynamic regime and in the physico-chemical parameter.

As expected, and seen in Fig. 17, the addition of ethanol with a lower boiling point than water increases the evaporation rate. The addition of surfactant increases the total surface area. However, it does not change the evaporation rate; which is consistent with above stated independence of the rate from the total surface area.

The stagnant layer cannot evaporate fully. Evaporation happens in the bulk fluid. The temperature of the stagnant layer is an average between bulk and surface. The surface of the stagnant later is at room temperature. Even seeing that the microjet plasma can be warm, it will be not warm enough to heat the stagnant layer significantly; expected to be only a few degrees. Lower boiling-point additives such as ethanol might evaporate to a certain degree, changing the surface composition of ethanol–water stagnant layer. Yet, expectation is that for the whole of the stagnant layer this is compensated by incoming ethanol flow from the bulk fluid. Evaporation can be suppressed when experiments are made at 100 % humidity in a controlled chamber environment around the plasma micro-jet, which is a plan for a future study on this subject.

This study explored a new reaction engineering concept to generate 'in-process' a thin, almost flat solvent layer out of a bulk liquid through impingement of a high-velocity gas jet (plasma). This layer is as thin as typical microfluidic flows for chemical processing. Specifically, the study demonstrates that a fixed bed at the floor of the reaction vessel can aid in improving the profile shape of the thin layer and deciphers how this is controlled by generic changes of physico-chemical parameters of the reaction solution. The fixed bed introduces catalytic capability, which, in addition to the liquid layer, can also interact with the gas phase, enabling 'plasma catalysis' in this study.

The variation of the physico-chemical parameters allowed to decipher which mechanisms rule the reaction investigated, the formation of NCQDs quantum dots from a dissolved precursor. This applies, in more general terms, also to a monomolecular reaction in a liquid phase that is non-thermally activated. The findings revealed that viscosity of the reaction solvent is the key factor governing mass transfer in the plasma-liquid system, with no impact on the reaction rate from changes in polarity, density, or surface tension unless combined with a viscosity change. Water, with the lowest viscosity, showed the highest reaction rate for NCQD synthesis. Solvent admixtures, including ethanol, that keep high viscosity but change other physico-chemical parameters (polarity, surface tension), reduce the reaction rate in aqueous media.

The total crater surface area and the specific surface area of the thinned solvent layer have no effect on the NCQDs reaction rate within the range of variations studied. This indicates that gaseous excited reaction species are in excess, that the plasma jet creates a single-point contact only, and the gas–liquid interfacial transition is not a limiting factor but the diffusion of the precursors toward the interface. The reaction occurs at the surface and doesn't penetrate the liquid, leaving diffusion within the liquid as the limiting factor.

Energy-wise, only 1.1 W is consumed for the micro jet plasma in this study, although 6 W is needed for gas pumping at a very high gas flow rate of 2 L/min, totalling 7 W. For comparison, plasma generation of an energy-intensified DBD plasma reactor takes 16 W and about 3 W for gas pumping at a flow rate of 0.14 L/min; totalling in 19 W [72,73]. This illustrates that the micro jet plasma is low in energy consumption, as the beforementioned DBD plasma reactor has been energy-optimised as compared to conventional plasma reactors, i.a., because of using nanopulses [73]. For comparison to another emerging technology (flow chemistry), pumping energy of a typical microreactor may consume 200 W.

Evaporation is an unavoidable consequence of applying a high-velocity jet to a solvent or solvent mixture. It is undesired as it transiently changes the reaction medium and exposes volatile, potentially toxic organic matter to the environment. Evaporation does not depend on the surface area of the entire crater, which demonstrates that evaporation happens only in the narrow area near the jet, which is almost constant under any experimental variation. As expected, evaporation depends on the boiling point of the solvent. Low-boiling solvents should be avoided, even as admixtures to a main solvent.

Understanding the fundamentals reported in this study opens paths for further studies. Future work should validate and elucidate the catalytic function and explore optimal catalyst design. For a broader and potentially larger impact of the new reactor engineering concept, tests with a very fast liquid reaction might exhibit a surface area dependence, impacting the interfacial transition of the excited species from the plasma phase. This study has already detailed surface area changes and their control. The new concept needs to be tested with reactions that do not impact polarity changes and might need to be stimulated in reaction rate by viscosity and density changes. Then the multitude of possible variations of surface profiles and hydrodynamics demonstrated in this study can be utilised for a broader application in synthetic chemistry, possibly even beyond plasma-chemical processing.

When varying process conditions in this study, we admittedly cannot rule out that the size, morphology, structure, and surface- and bulk-molecular composition of the produced nanomaterials of the produced nanomaterials may change. The needed investigations to clarify this would require an expansion of scope that is beyond what can be delivered within one study and one paper. We plan to address this in a future study.
